coredata.dc:title: Selective and ratiometric fluorescence sensing of bisphenol A in canned food based on portable fluorescent test strips
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiqing
Wang, Tao
Wang, Yongqing
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a conversion method and molecular imprinting technology were used to design molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP)-based ratiometric fluorescence test papers. The ZnO quantum dots (ZnO QDs) acted as the background quantum dots and ZIF-8 raw material. Carbon dots (CDs) were used as the identification signals. The imprinting layer achieved a selective function. Therefore, a ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs sensor was designed for the detection of Bisphenol A (BPA). The sensor exhibited a fast response time for BPA detection. In addition, the sensor demonstrated that effective detection of BPA can still be achieved in complex environments. The detection limit of this sensor was 0.778 nM with a linear range of 0–60 nM. The corresponding test solutions exhibited clear changes from blue to yellow. The selectivity experiments results demonstrated that ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs only exhibit excellent selective recognition effect for BPA. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was used for the detection of BPA in canned food and compared with the results of HPLC detection of BPA. The two spiked recovery ranges were 96.58–102.04% and 97.43–103.82%, respectively. In addition, the prepared ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 test paper visually recognized BPA under ultraviolet light. This study provides guidelines for the design and application of fluorescent test papers for quick detection in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bisphenol A
Conversion method
Fluorescence sensor
Fluorescent test paper
Ratiometric

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was maintained by Education Department of Jiangxi Province (GJJ211340, GJJ211339). We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of the as-prepared sample.
TEM images of CDs (a), ZnO QDs (b), ZnO@ZIF-8 (c), ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs (d), and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (e), and the SEM image of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (f).
XPS spectrum of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2(a), high-resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p (b), N 1s (c), C 1s (d), and O 1s (e).
N2 isotherms of the as-prepared samples.

FT-IR spectra of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 (a), FL intensity of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 before and after elution and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 (b, insert: the corresponding photos under an UV lamp).
Relative fluorescent intensity (a) and the response time to BPA of the fluorescent sensor (b).
Fluorescent spectra and corresponding photos of the fluorescent sensor with the addition of BPA (a–e) and the linear relationship (f).

SEM images of blank test paper (a) and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 fluorescent test paper (b), (c) images of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 test paper for detecting BPA (up: UV light illumination, down: sunlight illumination), and (d) the responding CIE chromaticity diagram.
Effect of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 ions (a), and the effects of BPA and some analogs of the fluorescent sensor (b).
Schematic of the preparation of the ratiometric fluorescence ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs sensor for BPA recognition.
Comparison of different fluorescence sensors for detecting BPA.
Detection of BPA spiked in real samples (n = 7).

head.ce:title: Selective and ratiometric fluorescence sensing of bisphenol A in canned food based on portable fluorescent test strips

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jingdezhen Ceramic University, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jingdezhen, 333403, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jingdezhen Ceramic University, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jingdezhen 333403, Peoples R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a conversion method and molecular imprinting technology were used to design molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP)-based ratiometric fluorescence test papers. The ZnO quantum dots (ZnO QDs) acted as the background quantum dots and ZIF-8 raw material. Carbon dots (CDs) were used as the identification signals. The imprinting layer achieved a selective function. Therefore, a ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs sensor was designed for the detection of Bisphenol A (BPA). The sensor exhibited a fast response time for BPA detection. In addition, the sensor demonstrated that effective detection of BPA can still be achieved in complex environments. The detection limit of this sensor was 0.778 nM with a linear range of 0–60 nM. The corresponding test solutions exhibited clear changes from blue to yellow. The selectivity experiments results demonstrated that ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs only exhibit excellent selective recognition effect for BPA. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was used for the detection of BPA in canned food and compared with the results of HPLC detection of BPA. The two spiked recovery ranges were 96.58–102.04% and 97.43–103.82%, respectively. In addition, the prepared ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 test paper visually recognized BPA under ultraviolet light. This study provides guidelines for the design and application of fluorescent test papers for quick detection in practical applications.

A ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs fluorescence sensor was constructed for selective and sensitive detecting bisphenol A (BPA) with naked-eye observation.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new fluorescence material, has been used for bioimaging, optical sensing, and catalysis owing to their low toxicity, biocompatibility, aqueous solubility, stability, and bight fluorescence [1,2]. Therefore, several original materials have been developed for preparing CDs, such as grass, glucose, and citric acid. For instance, Zulfajri et al. used a cheap and green carbon precursor to obtain CDs using an hydrothermal method [3]. Mao et al. used commercial lampblack to obtain fluorescent CDs that exhibited different responses to pH changes [4]. Although CDs have the potential to be used as fluorescence sensors, they are susceptible to external interference.

Recently, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor that can effectively alleviate environmental problems, excitation intensity, and concentration due to the fluorescence intensity changes induced by the harvesting of analytes from at least two emission bands has been developed [5–7]. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibited clear visualization effects. Wu et al. applied a ratiometric fluorescence sensor to detect N2H4 using naked eyes [8]. A dual lanthanide-doped ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibited a clear fluorescence color change via naked-eye observation [9]. Unfortunately, when constructing ratiometric fluorescence, the convergence of the two-quantum dot fluorescent materials interferes with the detection effect. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an effective approach to solve this problem.

To overcome this limitation, a suitable carrier is an effective measure to disperse nanomaterials and enhance their performance. Silicon spheres are traditionally used as carriers to disperse QDs materials. However, during the synthesis process, the silicon sphere does not have a rich pore structure, QDs agglomeration still occurs, and the thickness of the coated silicon is difficult to control [10]. Abundant porous materials are excellent carriers. Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs), a subclass of porous crystalline materials, have been extensively used in various applications [11–13]. Previous studies reported that ZIF-8 could be easily prepared using a conversion method, which could affect the crystallization kinetics and metal oxide dissolution [14–16]. Therefore, a conversion method was used to prepare ZIF-8 with ZnO QDs. ZnO QDs, as novel nanocrystals, not only exhibit outstanding sensing performance because of their relatively nontoxic properties and excellent optical and electrical properties but can also be converted to ZIF-8. This was a measure of killing two birds with one stone to construct ZIF-8 based ratiometric fluorescence sensors.

Moreover, a molecular imprinting technique should be applied to enhance the selectivity of the sensor. This method was used to easily coat a thinner layer of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) on the surface of MOFs [17,18]. In addition, MIP-grafted paper-based devices are more popular and convenient. Chen et al. demonstrated that paper-based sensors have the potential to detect tyramine in complex systems [19]. Cai et al. used test strips to detect Hg2+ ions in blood using copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) [20]. Thus, the fabrication of MIP-grafted test-strip-based fluorescence devices is necessary.

Based on the above analysis, a conversion method was used to prepare ZIF-8 using ZnO QDs. ZnO QDs were the metal source and reference signal, which formed a dual-emission fluorescence with CDs. Furthermore, MIPs were created to achieve selective recognition. Thus, a ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIP fluorescence sensor was constructed for the selective and sensitive detection of bisphenol A (BPA). In addition, a test paper-based fluorescence device was developed for detection. This study proposes a novel method for constructing a ZIF-8-baed fluorescence sensor using a conversion method.

Potassium hydroxide (KOH), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(Ac)2‧2H2O), 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2), methanol (CH3OH), and ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 4-Vinylpyridine (4-VP), 4-nonylphenol (NP), 4,4’-(butane-2,2′-diyl)diphenol (BPB), 4-octylphenon (OP), 4,4′-methylenediphenol (BPF), BPA, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), and 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AZBV) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All the reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

This process was performed according to a previous report [21]. Solution A: KOH (0.4859 g) to ethanol (23 mL). Solution B: 0.9790 g Zn(Ac)2‧2H2O in 42 mL ethanol. The resulting solution was poured into solution B and stirred for 10 min. Finally, a clear and transparent ZnO QDs solution with yellow fluorescence was obtained.

The ZnO QDs solution (5.0 mL) was dispersed in ethanol (5.0 mL) and ultrasound for 2.0 h. Thereafter, 2-methylimidazole ethanol solution (50 mL, 50 mg/mL) was added, and the pH was adjusted to 8.0. After 3.0 h of reaction at 50 °C, the sample was centrifuged and washed several times with ethanol. The solid was subsequently dried at 60 °C and denoted as ZnO@ZIF-8.

CDs were obtained from our previous report [22]. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs were prepared as follows: 50 mg of ZnO@ZIF-8 was dispersed ultrasonically in 20 mL of ethanol. Thereafter, the CDs solution was added and ultrasound for 6.0 h. The sample was dried to obtain ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs.

ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs were ultrasonically dispersed in a round-bottom flask containing 60 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, 10 mg of 4-VP and 5.0 mg of BPA were added and stirred for 6.0 h in the dark at room temperature. Next, EGDMA (28 μL) was added, and the mixture was saturated with N2 for 30 min. After adding AZBV saturated with N2 for 10 min, the reaction was sealed and stirred at 60 °C for 12 h. The sample was centrifuged and washed with eluent (volume ratio, methanol: ethanol = 9:1). The final sample was denoted as ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIP was obtained using the same process without the addition of BPA. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 were prepared by adjusting the reagent ratio (Table S1) using the same procedure.

The fluorescence measurements were conducted under similar conditions (excitation wavelength of 360 nm, 650 V photomultiplier tube voltage, and 10/10 nm slit width). Briefly, 0.5 mg/mL ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs were dispersed in ethanol and left unmixed for 30 min. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs stored solution (1.0 mL) and different concentrations of BPA solutions (10 μL) were added into colorimetric tubes and diluted to 5.0 mL constant volume. After standing for a long period of time, part of the mixture was transferred into a 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm cuvette for fluorescent detection.

The clean filter papers were fixed to 0.5 cm × 1.2 cm strips and immersed in 0.5 mg/mL ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs solutions for 10 min. Thereafter, the immersed strips were dropped into different concentrations of BPA solutions (10 μL) and photographed under ultraviolet (UV) illumination (λ
ex = 365 nm).

To investigate the practical applications of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs to recognize BPA in real samples, different brand-canned fish were purchased from a local campus store with some pretreatments, and spiking recovery experiments were conducted. In brief, 30 g of canned fish was dispersed in 20 mL of acetonitrile and ultrasonically extracted for 30 min. Thereafter, the precipitates were centrifuged and dispersed again in 10 mL of fresh acetonitrile. The second extracted solution was obtained through centrifugation and mixed with the first acetonitrile solution. Subsequently, 20 mL of hexane was added to the above solution with violent shaking, and the steps were repeated several times to remove some fat. Finally, the mixed extract was dried under nitrogen atmosphere and dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol.

The DA content was analyzed by 12,000 Agilent HPLC equipped with an Agilent ZORBAX Eclipse C18 Column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5.0 μm). The UV detector was set at 225 nm. The temperature of the remaining column was set at 35 °C. The mobile phase consisted of water and methanol (v: v = 13:87) at 1.0 mL/min flow rate.

The crystalline phases of the as-prepared samples were studied through XRD [23,24]. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the diffraction peaks of the ZnO QDs match well with those of ZnO (JCPDS No. 36–1451). No impurity peaks were observed for the ZnO QDs, indicating that pure ZnO QDs were obtained. After the addition of 2-methylimidazole, the ZnO peaks reduced; however, ZIF-8 peaks appeared, indicating the transformation of ZnO to ZIF-8. The results also demonstrated that ZnO QDs were anchored in situ on ZIF-8. The subsequent addition of CDs did not change the crystal structure of ZnO@ZIF-8 because of the excellent dispersion of CDs in the porous ZIF-8. As expected, only one amorphous peak appeared after the MIPs covered ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs. The MIPs were macromolecular polymers and amorphous; therefore, only one large amorphous peak was observed in the XRD pattern.

FT-IR spectra were used to further investigate the chemical structure (Fig. 1b). For ZnO QDs, the band at 480 cm−1 is attributed to the Zn–O bond of ZnO. The other peaks are the absorption peaks of the raw materials, such as the acid roots. After the conversion to ZIF-8, a sharp peak was observed at 415 cm−1 in ZnO@ZIF-8, which was attributed to the Zn–N stretching of ZIF-8. The peaks at 1306, 1145, and 759 cm−1 indicated the plane bending of the imidazole ring. The peak at 1444 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching of the imidazole ring. The other peaks at 2928 and 3133 cm−1 were attributed to the aromatic aliphatic C–H stretching. Owing to the formation of ZnO@ZIF-8, the typical peaks of Zn–O and O–H were weakened or they disappeared. This phenomenon was attributed to the formation of ZIF-8, which covered the ZnO QDs. The addition of CDs not only exhibited the characteristic peak of CDs on the FT-IR spectra but also the characteristic peaks of Zn–O appeared again. This phenomenon changed the morphology of the sample owing to the crystallization of the substance due to the addition of CDs. After the MIPs layer covered the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs, the typical peaks of macromolecular polymers were observed. These results demonstrated that ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was successfully designed.

The TEM and SEM images indicated the morphologies and sizes of the samples, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, both the CDs and ZnO QDs were smaller than 10 nm and were evenly dispersed. Because ZIF-8 was prepared using the conversion method using ZnO QDs, the samples were small and stacked (Fig. 2c). The TEM image illustrates that ZnO QDs were evenly dispersed in ZIF-8, confirming the XRD results. The ZIF-8 profile became clear after the addition of the CDs. The ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs were more dispersed (Fig. 2d), which could be attributed to the abundant functional groups of the CDs that promoted the dispersion of ZnO@ZIF-8. This phenomenon was confirmed by the infrared results. After the MIPs covered the surface of the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs, a thin layer was observed (Fig. 2e), demonstrating that ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was successfully prepared. The corresponding SEM image of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 exhibited similar results (Fig. 2f).

The XPS spectrum of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was used to investigate its elemental chemical states. Fig. 3 a shows that the elements of Zn, C, N, and O were exhibited in the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sample. The Zn 2p XPS spectra exhibited two peaks at 1021.4 and 1044 3 eV, which were assigned to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively [25] (Fig. 3b). Zn was attributed to ZnO QDs and ZIF-8. The binding energy of N 1s at 398.8 and 399.4 eV was attributed to C–N and CN, respectively (Fig. 3c). The N element was obtained from the raw material; 2-methylimidazole. The high-resolution C 1s spectra exhibited binding energies at 284.8 and 285.3 eV, which were ascribed to C–C/CC and CN, respectively (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3e shows the O 1s peaks at 531.5 and 532.5 eV, which were ascribed to the CO and C–O bonding states, respectively.

The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the samples were analyzed using N2 adsorption-desorption experiments [26]. As shown in Fig. 4 a, ZnO@ZIF-8 and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs exhibit type II isotherms with microporous structures. The BET surface area and pore volume of ZnO@ZIF-8 were determined as 1028.6 m2/g and 1.4715 cm3/g, respectively. The specific surface area and pore volume of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs were 1103.5 m2/g and 1.8507 cm3/g, respectively. In addition, the pore size distribution spectra indicated that the pore sizes of ZnO@ZIF-8 and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs hardly changed (Figs. S1a and b), indicating that the addition of nanosized CDs had little effect on the surface area, pore volume, and pore size. After coating the imprinted layer, the BET curves of the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 samples exhibited type III isotherms (Fig. 4b). The specific surface area and pore size of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 were 780.5 m2/g and 2.34 nm, and those of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 were 980.5 m2/g and 3.18 nm, respectively. The increase in the surface area and pore size of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 compared to those of the non-imprinted sample (Figs. S1c and d) is mainly due to the abundant template molecules remaining on the surface of the imprinted layer after elution. These results indicated that ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was successfully prepared with abundant recognition sites.

The template molecules were cleaned to facilitate target molecular identification. Comparing the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 5 a), similar groups of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 demonstrated that the template molecule was eluted. It was observed that the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 exhibited similar FL intensity, which further confirmed that the template molecules were eluted (Fig. 5b). After the addition of the target, the FL intensity significantly decreased, demonstrating that the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor could detect BPA. Correspondingly, the fluorescence color of the solutions changed significantly under UV light. The fluorescence of the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 solution before BPA elution was light yellow. After elution, the color was similar to that of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@ NIPs-2 (blue, Fig. 5b inset). These results indicate that the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor can be used for the fluorescence detection and visual identification of the target BPA.

Before exploring the sensitivity of the fluorescence sensor to BPA, the fluorescence stability and response time of the fluorescence sensor were investigated. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (0.5 mg/mL) was dispersed in an ethanol solution and sealed, and its fluorescence intensity was measured every week. The relative fluorescence intensity ratios of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 within 12 weeks [(I436/I540)/(I436/I540)0] are shown in Fig. 6 a, and the relative fluorescence intensity ratios were all approximately 1.0, indicating that the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor exhibits excellent stability for a long time. Similarly, the fluorescence stability of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1, and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 was tested. The results indicated that all synthesized materials exhibited excellent stability. The XRD patterns of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 before and after application are shown in Fig. S2. No clear changes were observed, further demonstrating that the fluorescence sensor exhibits better potential for practical application. Subsequently, the response times of the fluorescence sensors were compared under similar conditions. The relative fluorescence intensity of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 continued to decrease within 3.0 min of BPA addition and thereafter remained relatively stable, indicating that the optimal response time of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 to BPA was 3.0 min (Fig. 6b). In contrast, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs was in contact with BPA faster because there was no imprinting layer, and it was relatively stable after 2.0 min. Although the response speed of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs was faster, they did not exhibit a selective function without the combination of imprinting technologies. The response time of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 was close to that of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs, which may be due to incomplete formation of the imprinted layer. However, an imprinted layer that is significantly thick affects the response speed. The response time of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 was 5.0 min. Therefore, proper encapsulation of the imprinted layer has a significant impact on responsiveness.

After optimizing the test conditions, the fluorescence detection ability and visual recognition effect of the fluorescence sensor at different BPA concentrations were explored. As shown in Fig. 7 , there were two fluorescence emission peaks in the fluorescence spectrum, which were derived from the CDs (436 nm) and ZnO QDs (540 nm). At 60 nM, with increasing BPA concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 decreased gradually, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the internal standard ZnO QDs coated with ZIF-8 remained unchanged. Under UV light, the corresponding fluorescence color of the solution also exhibited a clear change from blue to yellow (Fig. 7c). However, with increasing BPA concentration, the change in fluorescence intensity of the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 sensor at 436 nm was not as clear as that of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 (Fig. 7d). This was due to the lack of recognition sites for BPA on the surface of the non-molecularly imprinted layer, and only part of the CDs in contact with BPA caused a slight fluorescence quenching. The color changes in the corresponding fluorescent photos under UV light were not well visualized. For ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs without the imprinted layer (Fig. 7a), although they still exhibited excellent detection ability, the internal standard was disturbed, which affected the fluorescence intensity of the internal standard and visual effect. As shown in Fig. 7b, the internal standard fluorescence intensity of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 was also disturbed, which may have been caused by the formation of an incomplete imprinting layer. Because the imprinted layer of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 was significantly thick (Fig. 7e), its detection ability was affected, indicating that the imprinted layer thickness has a significant influence on the detection performance.

When the BPA concentration range was 0–60 nM, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by determining the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity ratio of the two fluorescence emission peaks of the fluorescence sensor and the BPA concentration (Fig. 7f and Table S2). LOD is usually referred to as the detection limit, and does not use a blank sample for detection; it tests a low concentration of analyte samples and the lowest value that can be measured under certain probability conditions. The linear equation of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was -log[(I436/I540)/(I436/I540)0] = 0.00833[c] + 0.00216 (R2 = 0.998), with a detection limit of 0.7782 nM. The ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs lacking the imprinted layer were interfered with by BPA because of the interference of internal standard fluorescence. The correlation coefficient of the linear relationship after fitting was R2 = 0.9866, and the LOD was as high as 11.05 nM, which was 13.20 times higher than that of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 has a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9895 and a high LOD of 9.773 nM because the internal standard fluorescence was also interfered with by BPA, which was 11.56 times higher than that of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2 was significantly less effective in detecting BPA because of the lack of specific recognition sites for BPA in the imprinted layer. Thus, its detection limit was 14.05 times higher than that of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2. Although the ability of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 to detect BPA increased, the correlation coefficient of the corresponding linear equation was 0.9956, with a detection limit of 3.17 nM, The imprinting layer was significantly thick, resulting in a detection limit that was 3.07 times higher than that of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, and the response time that was prolonged by 0.67 times. These results demonstrated that ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 has a faster response time (3.0 min), the highest correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9982), and the lowest detection limit (0.7782 nM) for BPA. In addition, visual identification of fluorescent color changes was most pronounced under UV light.

Thereafter, a fluorescent test paper based on ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was prepared. By comparing the SEM images of the blank (Fig. 8 a) and fluorescent test papers (Fig. 8b), it was observed that a large amount of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 was loaded on the surface and pores of the test paper, indicating the successful preparation of the fluorescent test paper. The recognition effect of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 fluorescent test paper on BPA was explored. After dropping different concentrations of BPA on the fluorescent test paper, the test paper exhibited almost no difference under sunlight; however, the fluorescence color changed under the UV lamp (Fig. 8c), which was similar to the fluorescence color of the solution under the UV lamp (Fig. 7c insert). Moreover, the color coordinates corresponding to the colors of the fluorescent test strips with different BPA concentrations can also be directly read from the CIE-1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 8d). The above experimental results confirmed that the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 fluorescent test paper can sensitively detect and visually identify BPA.

In comparison with the reported fluorescence sensors (Table 1 ), although the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor did not exhibit the lowest sensitivity and a wider linear range for BPA analysis, no precious metals or toxic materials were used in ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, and the structure was more conducive to QDs loading and imprint coating, which exhibited significant potential for the development of fluorescent materials. Moreover, compared to traditional HPLC and other methods, fluorescence detection methods are simpler and faster. The designed fluorescent test strip increases the convenience of the fluorescence detection method and it is the development trend of future detection methods.

To explore the anti-interference ability and selective recognition effect of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, a variety of ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+) were tested in the environment. When different interfering ions (50 nM) were added to the solution (Fig. 9 a), only Ag+ and Cu2+ caused the fluorescence intensity ratio of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 to change slightly, which may be due to the possible electron transfer between Ag+ and Cu2+ in the abundant groups on the surface of the CDs. However, after adding 30 nM BPA, the fluorescence intensity ratio of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 changed significantly and was not disturbed by Ag+ and Cu2+. This result indicates that ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 can sensitively detect BPA in complex environments, mainly because the molecularly imprinted layer can protect CDs from external environment interference and the introduction of ZnO QDs as a reference signal.

Subsequently, the selective detection ability and visual recognition effect of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2, and ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 on BPA and its analogs were investigated. Taking BPB, BPF, NP, and OP as analogs, it was observed that the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor only exhibited a high recognition effect for BPA (Fig. 9b). In contrast, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@NIPs-2, due to the lack of imprinted recognition sites in the polymer layer, exhibited no significant difference in the recognition effect of BPA and its analogs. Because the surface of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs was not coated with an imprinted layer, the addition of BPA and its analogs reduced the fluorescence intensity ratio. ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 exhibited a similar ability to recognize BPA and its analogs, further indicating that the imprinted layer of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-1 was not completely formed and that the fluorescent material could not specifically and selectively recognize BPA. Although ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-3 with an imprinted layer that was significantly thick could selectively recognize BPA, the previous analysis results demonstrated that its detection limit and response time were not as good as those of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2. Therefore, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 exhibited the best performance in this experiment.

Scheme 1 illustrates the preparation of the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIP sensor and the recognition of BPA. First, a fluorescent ZnO@ZIF-8 carrier was synthesized using ZnO QDs as the Zn source. ZnO@ZIF-8 not only provided a stable reference fluorescence signal but was also used as a carrier to disperse the CDs to obtain a dual-emission fluorescence sensor. Thereafter, the imprinted layer covered the surface of the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs via precipitation polymerization. During this process, the functional monomer (4-VP) and BPA formed an imprinted layer via covalent bonding. After the template molecules were removed through solvent extraction, the imprinted layer left imprinted holes, which were associated with the shape, size, and functional group structure of BPA, were completely complementary. Thus, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs could selectively detect BPA. Moreover, ZnO QDs were completely encapsulated in ZIF-8, and their fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable; however, the fluorescence of CDs changed with BPA elution or binding to provide visual identification.

The feasibility of the practical application of the ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 sensor was explored by measuring BPA in canned fish samples. Because ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 could not directly detect the BPA content in the actual samples, spike recovery experiments were performed by adding different concentrations of BPA standard solutions to the samples (Table 2 ). Simultaneously, BPA residues in canned fish samples were detected by HPLC to confirm the reliability of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 detection of BPA. Fig. S3a shows the liquid chromatograms of the solvent and the 50 nM BPA standard solution. For BPA concentrations in the range of–5–200 nM, a standard curve y = 0.2158x-0.2769 (R2 = 0.9995) was drawn (Fig. S3c), according to the relationship between the peak area of BPA and the change in the BPA standard concentration (Fig. S3d). After several parallel experiments (Table 2), the recovery rates of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 for BPA detection were in the range of 96.58–102.04%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 4.38%. For the HPLC method, the spiked recoveries of medium BPA content ranged from 97.43% to 103.82%, and the RSD was less than 4.06%. These results demonstrated that the detection results of BPA by ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 are not significantly different from those of the HPLC method, indicating that it exhibits excellent reliability and repeatability for the detection of BPA in canned food.

In this study, a conversion method was used to prepare a ZnO@ZIF-8-based fluorescence sensor. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs, was designed and applied for selective and sensitive detection of BPA. This sensor exhibited a fast response time and stability. Even in complex environments, the sensor still achieved BPA detection. This sensor exhibited a linear BPA assay range of 0–60 nM with a LOD of 0.778 nM. In addition, ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIP fluorescent test paper was prepared and used for the rapid and selective identification of BPA. By comparing the detection results of ZnO@ZIF-8/CDs@MIPs-2 and HPLC methods for BPA, the recovery rates of the two methods in the actual samples were 96.58–102.04% and 97.43–103.82%, and the RSD values were less than 4.38% and 4.06%, respectively. Thus, the proposed strategy provides an excellent sensor for the sensitive and selective detection of BPA. In addition, a conversion method was used to design the MOF-based fluorescence sensor.
Xiqing Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Tao Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yongqing Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was maintained by Education Department of Jiangxi Province (GJJ211340, GJJ211339). We would like to thank Editage (www.editage.cn) for English language editing.
